fun_fact: Italy is home to more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country in the world — over 50!
Japan has over 6,800 islands, and its oldest company, Kongo Gumi, was a construction firm founded in the year 578 AD.
The Amazon rainforest in Brazil produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to one in ten of all species on Earth.
Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is home to the world's largest coral reef system, the Great Barrier Reef.
France is the world's most visited country, welcoming around 90 million tourists each year. The Eiffel Tower was built in just 2 years!
The ancient Egyptians invented many things we still use today, including paper (papyrus), toothpaste, and the 365-day calendar.
Canada has more lakes than all other countries in the world combined — over 2 million freshwater lakes!
India invented the game of chess, the number zero, and the decimal system. It also has the world's largest film industry by number of films produced.
Switzerland has four official languages — German, French, Italian, and Romansh — and has been at peace with all nations for over 200 years.
Kenya is home to the Great Rift Valley and the Masai Mara, where millions of wildebeest make the world's greatest wildlife migration every year.
Iceland has no army and is considered one of the world's most peaceful countries. You can see the Northern Lights there from September to March!
Mexico is where chocolate was first made — the ancient Aztecs used cocoa beans as currency and drank chocolate as a sacred beverage.
New Zealand was the first country in the world to give women the right to vote, way back in 1893!
The Netherlands has about 23 million bicycles for a population of 17 million people — there are more bikes than people!
Greece has over 6,000 islands, though only about 200 are inhabited. Athens is considered the birthplace of democracy, philosophy, and the Olympic Games.
Norway has so many fjords that its coastline, if straightened out, would stretch halfway around the Earth. It also invented the paper clip!
Argentina is the birthplace of the tango dance, and it has the largest glacier outside of Antarctica — the Perito Moreno Glacier.
Thailand is the world's largest exporter of rice and has over 40,000 Buddhist temples. The word 'Thai' means 'free' in the Thai language.
Ireland is called the Emerald Isle because of its lush green landscape. Halloween is believed to have originated from the ancient Celtic festival of Samhain in Ireland.
Peru is home to Machu Picchu, the famous Inca citadel, and the potato — which was first cultivated in Peru thousands of years ago.
Portugal is the oldest nation-state in Europe, with borders that have been unchanged since 1139. Cork from Portuguese trees provides the stoppers for most of the world's wine bottles.
South Africa has three capital cities — Pretoria, Cape Town, and Bloemfontein — and is the only country in Africa that produces wine commercially.
Scotland's national animal is the unicorn! Scottish people also invented the telephone (Alexander Graham Bell), the television (John Logie Baird), and penicillin (Alexander Fleming).
China invented paper, printing, gunpowder, and the compass — four inventions that changed the course of world history.
Russia is the largest country in the world — it covers 11 time zones! It also has Lake Baikal, the world's deepest and oldest freshwater lake.
Morocco is home to the University of al-Qarawiyyin in Fez, founded in 859 AD — the oldest continuously operating university in the world.
Denmark is home to LEGO, the world's most popular toy brand! The word 'LEGO' comes from the Danish phrase 'leg godt', meaning 'play well'.
Colombia is the only country in South America with coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea. It produces over half of the world's emeralds.
Turkey is where the world's first cathedral was built, and the city of Istanbul is the only city in the world that spans two continents — Europe and Asia.
Jamaica was the first Caribbean country to compete in the Winter Olympics, and it is the birthplace of reggae music, made famous by Bob Marley.
Finland has the most saunas per person in the world — about one sauna for every three people! It is also home to Santa Claus's official village in Lapland.
Spain is home to La Tomatina, the famous annual tomato-throwing festival, and flamenco — a passionate dance and musical tradition born in Andalusia.
Vietnam has over 3,000 km of coastline and is one of the world's leading producers of coffee. Ha Long Bay, with its stunning emerald waters, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Austria gave the world Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, who composed his first piece of music at age five! Vienna is famous as a city of classical music.
Ghana was the first sub-Saharan country to gain independence in 1957 and is one of the world's largest producers of cocoa and gold.
Sweden gave us IKEA, Spotify, Minecraft, and Volvo — plus the tradition of 'fika', a daily coffee-and-cake break that is a beloved national institution.
Israel is home to the Dead Sea — the lowest point on Earth's surface — where the water is so salty that you float effortlessly without trying.
Cuba is known as the 'Pearl of the Caribbean' and is the largest island in the Caribbean. It is famous for its salsa music, colourful classic cars, and hand-rolled cigars.
Hungary invented the Rubik's Cube — that colourful puzzle that stumped the world in the 1980s! Budapest was formed by joining three cities in 1873.
The Philippines is an archipelago of over 7,600 islands and is home to the world's smallest primate — the Philippine tarsier, which is small enough to fit in your hand.
The Czech Republic has the highest beer consumption per capita in the world. Prague's old town has been almost perfectly preserved since medieval times.
Nepal is home to 8 of the world's 10 highest mountains, including Mount Everest — the tallest peak on Earth at 8,849 metres above sea level.
Tanzania is home to Kilimanjaro, Africa's highest mountain, and the Serengeti — where over a million wildebeest roam in one of the greatest wildlife spectacles on Earth.
Poland gave the world Marie Curie, the first woman to win a Nobel Prize — and she won it twice! Poland is also famous for its pierogi dumplings and amber jewellery.
Ecuador is named after the Equator, which runs right through it! It is home to the Galápagos Islands, where Darwin developed his theory of evolution.
Belgium invented French fries (despite the name!), waffles, and some of the world's finest chocolates. It also produces over 1,000 different varieties of beer.
Bhutan is the only country in the world that measures its success by 'Gross National Happiness' rather than economic output. Plastic bags are completely banned there.
Croatia invented the necktie — the word 'cravat' comes from 'Croat'. It also has the beautiful Plitvice Lakes, where turquoise waterfalls cascade between 16 terraced lakes.
Ethiopia is the birthplace of coffee — legend says a goat herder discovered the plant after his goats ate it and became energetic. It uses a unique 13-month calendar!
Sri Lanka is the world's largest exporter of cinnamon and is shaped like a teardrop in the Indian Ocean. It has over 1,500 species of flowering plants.
